input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: Infrastructure within the Architecture, Engineering, Construction and Operations (AECO) domain, represented as a digital twin through integration of static models and runtime data for simulation, analysis, and optimization, with buildings as a primary example.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides real-time monitoring insights, predictive analytics for forecasting, and infrastructure optimization services to stakeholders in the Architecture, Engineering, Construction and Operations (AECO) domain, enabling reduced operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved collaboration across the building lifecycle from initial design to operational refurbishments.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation leverages Eclipse Hono for IoT data ingestion and device management, Eclipse Ditto for digital twin instance management and state synchronization, Unreal Engine for photorealistic 3D visualization and simulation, and Building Information Modeling (BIM) for static asset representation and spatial data integration.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Modular architecture with standardized extension points enabling integration of custom services and analytics; DT Framework acts as a central hub facilitating bidirectional data exchange, with roles including Building Control Engineers for device modeling, Facility Managers for real-time monitoring and control, and DT Engineers for service integration to support holistic building lifecycle management.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Nonfunctional requirements (NF_X) address interoperability across heterogeneous systems via standardized extension points for service integration; specific data exchange protocols or standards are not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: BIM-based device modeling of sensors, actuators, and controllers with a unified semantic foundation; Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles extract structured representations to generate deployable DT artifacts. Real-time data exchange synchronizes physical building data with the digital twin.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The framework includes model validation processes specified in Section IV and utilizes the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric to assess the accuracy of the digital twin's representation of its physical counterpart.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Real-time bidirectional data exchange enables continuous synchronization between physical building systems and their digital counterparts. The framework utilizes a modular architecture with standardized extension points for integrating custom services and analytics to enrich BIM models with operational data from physical assets.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle includes initial device modeling using BIM for static representation, followed by Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) transformation into structured semantic representations, automatic generation of deployable DT artifacts, real-time data synchronization between physical and digital systems, interactive visualization for monitoring and control, and modular extension through custom services. Governance is managed via role-specific responsibilities: Building Control Engineers model devices and configure control logic, Facility Managers monitor system states and operate infrastructure, and DT Engineers develop custom services.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) is employed to extract structured representations from enriched BIM models and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts. Evolution is managed via a modular architecture with standardized extension points for integrating custom services and analytics to adapt to operational changes.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Interactive visualization enables Facility Managers to monitor system states and control physical infrastructure. Custom analytics services can be integrated through standardized extension points, though specific insight types, decision-making processes, and confidence measures are not detailed in the document.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately captures the definition of the system described in the introduction, identifying it as infrastructure in the AECO domain and buildings as a specific example. It correctly cites the integration of static models and runtime data for simulation and optimization. Supported by quotes: 'creates a virtual representation of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization' and 'architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO) domain'.

The extraction is highly specific and directly supported by the source text. It accurately lists services such as real-time monitoring, predictive analytics, and optimization, and correctly identifies the domain (AECO) and benefits (reduced costs, enhanced decision-making). The extraction is a near-verbatim summary of the capabilities described in the Introduction.

Accurately identifies the specific tools and their roles as described in the source. Supported by quotes: 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange', 'Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization', and 'grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM)... static planning artifacts' and 'model backbone for spatial and semantic data integration'. The extraction correctly maps the technologies to their functions in the framework.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The provided text describes real-time capabilities and live synchronization but does not provide specific details on synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, or temporal granularity needs.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source text focuses on a model-driven deployment framework and stakeholder roles but does not discuss multiple digital twin instances, centralized versus decentralized architectures, or coordination mechanisms between twins.

The description accurately summarizes the system's orchestration, architecture, and roles. It correctly identifies the modular architecture with extension points ('standardized extension points to enable modular, service-oriented DT platforms'), the DT Framework as a central hub ('The DT Framework acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange'), and the specific roles ('Building Control Engineers', 'Facility Managers', 'DT Engineers') found in Table I and the text.

The description correctly notes that interoperability is addressed via non-functional requirements and standardized extension points ('nonfunctional requirements (NF_X) address critical aspects of interoperability across heterogeneous systems'), and accurately observes that specific data exchange protocols are not detailed in the text ('Real-time data exchange using Eclipse Hono and Ditto' is mentioned, but specific protocols are not).

block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extracted characteristic is highly specific and accurately reflects the source text. It correctly identifies the use of BIM for device modeling with a unified semantic foundation, the use of MDE principles for extracting representations and generating artifacts, and the role of real-time data exchange. Quotes supporting this include: 'define sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a unified semantic foundation', 'leverages MDE principles to extract structured representations... automatically generate deployable DT artifacts', and 'Real-time data integration... enabling live synchronization between physical assets and digital twins'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The provided source text mentions 'actuators' and 'control logic' only in the context of modeling them within the framework or the need for bidirectional communication (e.g., 'define sensors, actuators, and controllers', 'feed control signals back to the BCS'). It does not describe the physical actuators themselves, their specific types, actuation ranges, or safety constraints as required by the characteristic definition. Therefore, the assessment that the detailed characteristic is missing is correct.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source text mentions 'sensor configurations' and 'sensor data' (e.g., 'post-occupancy sensor data', 'sensor feedback') and modeling sensors in BIM. However, it does not provide details on sensor types, spatial distribution, sampling frequencies, or accuracy specifications required by the characteristic definition. Thus, stating it is 'Not in Document' is an accurate evaluation of the provided text.

The characteristic accurately captures the validation process mentioned in the text and correctly identifies the specific metric (ODTE) used to evaluate the twin's accuracy. It also correctly links the validation process to Section IV. Supported by quotes: 'evaluate the entanglement of the resulting DT using the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric' and 'after validating the model [65], the model is fed into the Hono/Ditto Configurator'.

block_5_judge.reasoning: The description accurately captures the lifecycle stages: initial modeling with BIM, MDE transformation, automatic generation, real-time synchronization, and visualization. It correctly identifies the governance roles for Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, and DT Engineers. Supported by quotes: 'Our approach automates the extraction of domain semantics and the generation of runtime interfaces' and 'Table I: Actor Roles... Building Control Engineer... Facility Manager... DT Engineer'.

The description correctly identifies MDE as the methodology for extracting representations and generating artifacts. It accurately describes evolution via a modular architecture with extension points. Evidence includes: 'R1: A modeling methodology to standardize BIM semantics for a model-driven transformation pipeline' and 'R4: A modular architecture with defined extension points to support scalable, service-oriented DT development'.

The description correctly states that visualization enables monitoring and control and that custom analytics can be integrated via extension points. It correctly notes the lack of specific details on insight types. Supported by: 'Unreal Engine 5 provides a rich visualization environment for interactive monitoring and simulation' and 'Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts with the twin, and operates the physical infrastructure via the DT'.

The document mentions 'standardized integration frameworks' and 'standardized extension points', but does not specify compliance with particular industry standards, certifications, or interoperability standards (e.g., ISO, IEC). Therefore, stating that specific standardization details are 'Not in Document' is correct.

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
